segments.paradiso-25-g70: Da molte stelle places the source prepositional phrase at the start for emphasis. mi is a dative pronoun ('to me') and vien is the present singular (poetic/archaic) form of viene, so the clause literally reads 'from many stars to me comes this light' — the subject questa luce follows the verb, a typical inversion for poetic effect.
segments.paradiso-25-g71: ma introduces a contrast. quei is an archaic demonstrative/pronoun ('that one' / 'he') serving as the subject; la is a feminine direct object referring back to luce. distillò is passato remoto (3rd sg.) indicating a completed past action, used figuratively here ('distilled' = extracted or instilled). nel mio cor uses nel = in/into + mio cor = 'into my heart.' pria che introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'before ...', followed by fu (passato remoto of essere) — Dante uses the indicative here where later usage might prefer a subjunctive — so 'before he was.' sommo cantor del sommo duce is a genitive phrase apposition 'the supreme singer of the supreme leader.'
segments.paradiso-25-g72: 'Sperino' is the 3rd-person plural present subjunctive (or jussive) of sperare, used here as a hortatory/exhortatory statement in direct quotation: 'may they hope' / 'let them hope'. 'in te' is the preposition + clitic object 'in you' (i.e. 'in you [they should place their] hope'), with normal word order Verb + prepositional object because it's quoted speech.
segments.paradiso-25-g73: Ne = 'in' or 'within' here introduces the environment where the utterance occurs ('in her/their tëodia'); 'la sua tëodia' is a possessive + noun (sua = 'his/her/its') and is a poetic/possibly obscure noun (tëodia). 'dice' is present indicative 3rd singular 'he/she/it says' and links the quoted clause to its speaker.
segments.paradiso-25-g74: 'color' is a plural demonstrative-noun 'those (people)'; 'che sanno il nome tuo' is a relative clause modifying 'color' ('who know your name'). 'Sanno' is present indicative 3rd plural of sapere. The order 'il nome tuo' is slightly marked (noun + possessive) typical of Danteic/poetic syntax.
segments.paradiso-25-g75: E chi nol sa = 'and who does not know it': 'chi' is the indefinite/whoever-subject, 'nol' is the clitic contraction of 'non lo' (the negative + direct object clitic) placed before the verb (archaic order), and 'sa' is present indicative 3rd singular of sapere.
segments.paradiso-25-g76: S'elli ha la fede mia? is an archaic conditional/elliptical question: 's'' stands for 'se' ('if'), 'elli' is an archaic subject pronoun meaning 'he' (or 'one'), and 'ha' is present indicative ('has'); the phrase asks 'if he has my faith?' The whole is a rhetorical question depending on the preceding 'chi nol sa'.
segments.paradiso-25-g77: Tu mi stillasti: 'Tu' is the subject 'you'; 'mi' the direct object 'me'; 'stillasti' is a past (poetic/archaic) 2nd-person singular form of stillare/stillastare ('you dripped/distilled'), describing a completed action performed by 'you' upon the speaker.
segments.paradiso-25-g78: Con lo stillar suo = 'with her/its dripping': 'con' marks the means; 'lo stillar' is an infinitive used nominally ('the dripping' / 'the act of dripping'); 'suo' is a possessive referring back to the female/thing previously mentioned (her/its). This phrase specifies the manner by which the speaker was 'distilled'.
segments.paradiso-25-g79: Ne la pistola poi: 'ne' here functions like 'in it' / 'into' (referring back to the receiving vessel), 'la pistola' is the noun 'the pistola' (an older/poetic term for a small container/vessel in this context), and 'poi' means 'afterward' or 'then'. The phrase places the dripping into a vessel that follows in sequence.
segments.paradiso-25-g80: Sì ch' io son pieno = 'so that I am full': 'sì che' introduces a result clause ('so that'); the contraction 'ch'' = 'che' ('that'); 'io' is the first-person subject; 'son' is the present indicative 'I am'; 'pieno' is the predicate adjective 'full'.
segments.paradiso-25-g81: E in altrui vostra pioggia repluo: 'e' = 'and'; 'in altrui' = 'into others' / 'among others' (altrui = someone else's / other people); 'vostra pioggia' = 'your rain' (vostra qualifying pioggia); 'repluo' is 1st-person present 'I flow back / I pour back' (re- + pluo), so the speaker says that, being filled, he overflows/returns as the addressee's rain upon others.
segments.paradiso-25-g82: Mentr' io diceva = 'While I was saying (it)': 'Mentr'' is the contracted form of 'mentre' ('while'); 'io' is the subject; 'diceva' is the imperfect indicative 1st singular ('I was saying'), marking an ongoing past action that provides the temporal frame for what follows.
segments.paradiso-25-g83: This entire phrase is a poetic word order: the locative phrase 'dentro al vivo seno di quello incendio' ('within the living breast of that fire') precedes the verb; 'tremolava' is imperfect 3rd singular 'flickered/quivered'; the grammatical subject 'un lampo' ('a flash') comes after the verb (inversion typical of poetry). The final modifiers 'sùbito e spesso a guisa di baleno' describe the flash as 'sudden and frequent, in the manner of lightning' — 'a guisa di' = 'in the manner of'.
segments.paradiso-25-g28: Ridendo is a gerund (adverbial participle) meaning "laughing," placed before the main verb to indicate manner. allora is a temporal adverb "then/thereupon." Bëatrice is the subject; disse is passato remoto (simple past) of dire, "said," introducing direct speech.
segments.paradiso-25-g29: Inclita vita is a vocative phrase ('renowned/illustrious life') addressing someone or something personified. per cui = 'through/by whom' (relative phrase indicating agency). la larghezza de la nostra basilica is a noun phrase 'the largeness/abundance of our basilica.' si scrisse uses reflexive/passive si with scrisse (passato remoto of scrivere) to mean 'was written/was composed.'
segments.paradiso-25-g30: fa is an imperative-like present of fare here used to give a command ('make' or 'cause'). la spene = 'the hope' (archaic spelling of speranza). in questa altezza = 'at/within this height/heightened place' (altezza = loftiness or this high sphere), indicating the place where hope should "resound."
segments.paradiso-25-g31: tu sai is a simple present indicative, 'you know.' The pronoun tu is emphatic here because Beatrice is addressing the addressee directly.
segments.paradiso-25-g32: che introduces a subordinate clause 'that/as' clarifying what the addressee knows. tante fiate = 'so many times' (tante = 'so many'; fiate is an archaic plural meaning 'occasions/times'). la figuri is 2nd-person singular (tu) present (or present-subjunctive/poetic form) of figurar/figurare used here as 'you represent/portray/imagine it' (la = the thing being represented).
segments.paradiso-25-g33: quante = 'as many as.' Iesù = 'Jesus.' ai tre = 'to the three' (a + il). fé is an archaic/dialectal past form of fare used here roughly as 'gave/made' (i.e., 'bestowed'); più modifies carezza ('more/greater caress' or 'greater affection'). The whole clause gives the measure: 'as many as Jesus gave the three greater caresses.'
segments.paradiso-25-g34: Leva la testa is an imperative phrase: leva (from levare) = 'lift' and la testa = 'the head.' e fa che t’assicuri uses fa (imperative/causative 'make' or 'see that') plus a subordinate clause with assicur[i] in the subjunctive (t’assicuri) meaning 'that you be assured' or 'make sure for yourself.' The construction fa che + subjunctive is a causative construction meaning 'ensure that...'.
segments.paradiso-25-g35: che ciò che vien qua sù del mortal mondo is a nominal subordinate clause introduced by che: ciò che = 'that which/whatever'; vien (viene) = present 'comes' or 'arrives'; qua sù = 'here up' (this high place); del mortal mondo = 'from the mortal world.' Together: 'that whatever comes up here from the mortal world...' The verb vien is indicative describing what actually arrives from below.
segments.paradiso-25-g36: convien is an impersonal present (from convenire) meaning 'it is required/it is necessary.' ch' = che introduces the subordinate clause; ai nostri raggi 'by/under our rays' (ai = a + il); si maturi uses si plus the present subjunctive maturi (from maturare) in an impersonal/passive sense: 'must ripen/mature' — the construction expresses necessity that what arrives be ripened by their radiance.
segments.paradiso-25-g37: Questo conforto = 'this comfort/encouragement' (subject); del foco secondo = 'of the second fire/light' (genitive phrase identifying which comfort); mi venne = 'came to me' — venne is a past tense (narrative past), so the clause states that this comfort arrived to the narrator.
segments.paradiso-25-g38: Ond’ (onde) = 'whereupon/therefore/from which' linking consequence; io leväi li occhi a’ monti = 'I raised my eyes to the mountains' (leväi is the past tense 'I raised'); the relative clause che li ’ncurvaron pria col troppo pondo = 'which had earlier bowed (themselves) with too great weight' — ncurvaron is a past tense (they bowed/bent), col = con + il 'with the', troppo pondo = 'too great a weight.'
segments.paradiso-25-g39: Poi che = 'since/now that' introducing a causal clause; per grazia = 'by/through grace' (explaining motive); vuol che introduces a subordinate clause and governs the subjunctive; tu t’affronti is a present subjunctive (tu + reflexive clitic t’) of affrontare meaning 'you should present yourself / come before / face' — lo nostro Imperadore is the person to be faced.
segments.paradiso-25-g40: Anzi = 'nay/rather' used to correct or intensify the preceding term; la morte = 'Death' presented as the more precise identification (i.e. 'our Emperor, rather Death').
segments.paradiso-25-g41: Ne l’aula più secreta = 'in the most secret hall' (ne = in; più secreta = most secret); co’ suoi conti = 'with his counts/councillors' (co' = contraction of con + vowel; conti here means noble companions/counselors), indicating the place and company where the presentation should take place.
segments.paradiso-25-g42: Sì che = 'so that' or 'with the result that' introducing the consequence/result clause that follows.
segments.paradiso-25-g43: Veduto (past participle) + noun phrase = 'having seen'; veduto il ver di questa corte = 'having seen the truth of this court' (veduto is a perfect participle forming a temporal/causal subordinate idea).
segments.paradiso-25-g44: La spene = 'the hope' (spene = hope); this is the topic of the following relative clause and of the request to the narrator: the hope that will be affected by what he is to report.
segments.paradiso-25-g45: Che là giù bene innamora = relative clause 'which there below well enamors/enchants' — che = 'which'; là giù = 'down there / below' (adverbial); bene = 'well'; innamora (present) = 'enchants/makes fall in love' (active sense: it inspires love or strong desire).
segments.paradiso-25-g46: In te e in altrui di ciò conforte = 'may it strengthen/comfort in you and in others with regard to this' — in te e in altrui = 'in you and in others'; di ciò = 'about/with respect to this'; conforte is a present/subjunctive/optative form meaning 'may it comfort/encourage' (Dante uses this form to express the hoped-for effect).
segments.paradiso-25-g47: Di’ (imperative of dire) = 'Say / Tell'; quel ch’ell’ è = 'what it is' (quel = 'that which', ch’ = 'that/which', ell' = 'it/she', è = 'is') — a direct imperative request to state the nature of that hope.
segments.paradiso-25-g48: Di’ come = 'Say how' (di’ again imperative); se ne ’nfiora la mente tua = 'your mind blossoms/flowers forth from it' — come introduces manner; se (here part of the verbal phrase) + ne (of it/from it) + ’nfiora (poetic verb 'to flower/put forth bloom') means 'how from that (it) your mind is made to blossom/brighten'; la mente tua = 'your mind.'
segments.paradiso-25-g49: E dì = 'and say' (coordinating imperative); onde a te venne = 'from whence it came to you' (onde = 'from which/whence', a te = 'to you', venne = past tense 'came'), requesting the narrator to state the source of that impression/hope.
segments.paradiso-25-g1: This is the protasis introduced by the conditional marker Se mai continga 'If ever it should happen'. Continga is the present subjunctive of contingere (here 'to happen'); the relative clause al quale ha posto mano e cielo e terra modifies 'poema sacro' and uses ha posto mano ('has put a hand') as an idiom meaning 'have taken part / lent a hand'. The article contraction ’l = il precedes poema; al quale is a relative pronoun referring back to 'poema'.
segments.paradiso-25-g2: Sì che here introduces the result ('so that'). M’ha fatto is a compound tense (ha fatto) with the clitic m’ = mi 'made me', and per molti anni gives the duration 'for many years'. Macro is an archaic adjective meaning 'lean' or 'emaciated' describing the speaker's state produced by that making.
segments.paradiso-25-g3: Vinca is the present subjunctive of vincere used in the apodosis of the conditional clause begun by Se...continga 'If... should happen'. La crudeltà is the grammatical subject; che fuor mi serra is a relative clause: fuor 'outside' + mi serra (serrare, 'to shut/keep') meaning 'that keeps me out'. Del bello ovile introduces the place 'from the beautiful sheepfold' and ov' io dormi’ uses the archaic ov' (ove/where) with dormi' (passato remoto dormii) and agnello in apposition or predicative sense 'where I slept (as) a lamb'.
segments.paradiso-25-g4: Nimico ai lupi is a nominal phrase 'enemy to the wolves' with ai = a + i. The relative clause che li danno guerra uses present indicative (danno) and li as the object referring back to the flock/people; danno guerra is an idiom 'make war on / assail'. The whole phrase further characterizes the sheepfold and speaker's relation to the wolves.
segments.paradiso-25-g5: Con altra voce omai: con + noun expresses 'with' or 'by means of' another voice; omai is an adverb meaning 'now / henceforth' (here: henceforth or from now on). This phrase signals a change of manner or style.
segments.paradiso-25-g6: Con altro vello is literal 'with another fleece' (a metaphor for a different style or guise). Ritornerò is future first person 'I will return' and poeta is a predicative noun: 'I will return [as] a poet.' The future tense signals a resolved intention or promise contingent on the earlier condition.
segments.paradiso-25-g7: E in sul fonte del mio battesmo prenderò ’l cappello: e 'and'; in sul (archaic) = in + su 'on/at the'; fonte 'font' or 'source'; prenderò is future 'I will take' and ’l cappello uses the article + noun 'the cap/hat'. Together this says he will take the hat at the font of his baptism (a symbolic gesture) in the future.
segments.paradiso-25-g8: Però che ne la fede: però che introduces an explanatory clause 'because/for that reason that'; ne = in (archaic form used before la), so ne la fede = 'in the faith'. This begins a clause explaining why he will take the cap.
segments.paradiso-25-g9: Che fa conte l’anime a Dio: che 'which' (referring to 'fede'), fa is the verb 'makes' or 'causes', conte is 'count' or 'reckoning' (here 'make account'), and l’anime a Dio means 'the souls before God' — together: 'the faith that makes the souls be reckoned to God' (i.e. the faith by which souls are counted to God).
segments.paradiso-25-g10: Quivi intra’ io: quivi 'there', intra’ is an archaic past form (passato remoto) of intrare 'I entered/entered in', and io is an emphatic subject pronoun. Word order places the locative adverb first for emphasis: 'there I entered.'
segments.paradiso-25-g11: E poi Pietro per lei sì mi girò la fronte: e poi 'and then'; Pietro is the agent; per lei 'for it / because of it' (lei = the faith, feminine); mi is the indirect object 'to me'; girò is passato remoto of girare 'turned'; la fronte is the direct object 'the forehead'. Literally: 'and then Peter, because of it, turned my forehead.'
segments.paradiso-25-g12: Indi 'then'; si mosse is the past reflexive construction (mosse, passato remoto of muovere, with si) meaning 'moved' (literally 'moved itself' → 'moved toward us'); un lume verso noi 'a light toward us'. Di quella spera uses di + demonstrative to mean 'from that sphere' (spera/sfera in poetic spelling); ond' uscì 'whence issued' (ond' = whence) and la primizia 'the firstfruit' is the subject of the following relative clause che lasciò Cristo d’i vicari suoi 'which Christ left to his vicars'.
segments.paradiso-25-g13: E la mia donna: simple nominative phrase 'and my lady' with la mia donna (Beatrice). The article plus possessive is the normal way to say 'my lady' in Italian/poetic usage.
segments.paradiso-25-g14: Piena di letizia is an adjectival phrase 'full of joy' where piena (feminine to agree with donna) + di + letizia expresses the emotion with which she speaks.
segments.paradiso-25-g15: Mi disse: 'she said to me' — mi is dative clitic 'to me', disse is passato remoto of dire 'said', introducing direct speech.
segments.paradiso-25-g16: Mira is an imperative (second person singular) 'look' or 'behold' used to draw attention; the vocative or command tone suits the following exclamation.
segments.paradiso-25-g17: The repeated mira: again an imperative or exhortation 'look', intensifying the command to observe what follows.
segments.paradiso-25-g18: Ecco il barone 'here is the nobleman': ecco 'behold/here is'; il barone 'the baron/nobleman'. Per cui 'for whom / on account of whom'; là giù 'down there' (la + giù), si is the reflexive/impersonal clitic, and vicita is an archaic/poetic verb form (contextually 'is visited/affected/concerned' — here left as literal), followed by the place name Galizia.
segments.paradiso-25-g19: Sì come quando introduces a simile clause 'just as when'. Il colombo 'the dove' is the subject; si pone (present indicative) is reflexive 'settles/places itself'. Presso al compagno uses presso + article to mean 'near its companion', with the article contraction al = a + il.
segments.paradiso-25-g20: L’uno a l’altro pande: literal word order 'the one to the other spreads' (i.e. each bends/spreads toward the other). Pande is the present tense of pandere 'to spread/extend' and the phrasing l'uno a l'altro is a reciprocal expression.
segments.paradiso-25-g21: Girando e mormorando are gerunds 'turning and murmuring' that describe the manner of their action; gerunds are used here to show simultaneous, ongoing behavior and to create a vivid image.
segments.paradiso-25-g22: L’affezione: the noun with the definite article 'the affection' or 'the attachment' — the result or name of the action just described (what spreads between them).
segments.paradiso-25-g23: Così vid’ ïo ... essere accolto: così 'thus'; vid' is an archaic contracted passato remoto of vedere (vidi) 'I saw', with ïo an emphatic subject pronoun 'I'. The structure vid(i) + infinitive (essere accolto) is a perception construction meaning 'I saw ... being received'; l’uno da l’altro marks the agent/source 'one by the other' and the sequence describes one great prince received by another glorious prince.
segments.paradiso-25-g24: Laudando il cibo che là sù li prande: laudando (gerund) 'praising' modifies the princes; il cibo 'the food'; che introduces a relative clause: là sù 'there above' (locative), li = 'them' (clitic) and prande is a poetic verb (from prandere) meaning 'feeds' or 'provides food to' — 'praising the food that there above feeds them.'
segments.paradiso-25-g25: Ma poi che = 'But after' (temporal/concessive conjunction); 'l gratular' uses the infinitive as a masculine noun meaning 'the greeting/congratulation'; 'si fu assolto' is a periphrastic perfect/passive-like construction (auxiliary fu + past participle assolto) with reflexive si, meaning 'had been completed/fulfilled.'
segments.paradiso-25-g26: Tacito = adjectival/poetic use meaning 'silent'; coram me = Latin prepositional phrase 'before me / in my presence'; ciascun is the singular distributive 'each one'; s’affisse is a reflexive past form (here a discrete past action) meaning 'set himself/held himself fixed' or 'fell silent and fixed himself' — the reflexive clitic s’ attaches to the verb and ciascun is its subject.
segments.paradiso-25-g27: Ignito is a past-participle/adjectival form meaning 'inflamed' or 'kindled' describing their state; sì che introduces a result clause 'so that'; vincëa is an imperfect form meaning 'overcame/overwhelmed' (here 'overcame' in the sense of affecting the speaker); '’l mio volto' is the direct object 'my face' — together: 'so inflamed that it overwhelmed my face.'
segments.paradiso-25-g126: A questa voce is a prepositional phrase meaning "at this voice/word(s)" (A = at). l’infiammato giro is the subject: the article + adjective + noun (the "inflamed/ardent ring/circle"). si quïetò is a reflexive past action (si + quïetò, 3rd person singular past) meaning "calmed itself"; the diaeresis on ï signals separate vowel sounds in the poetry. con esso il dolce mischio introduces what calmed together with it: con esso = "with it/therewith," il dolce mischio = "the sweet mingling," and che si facea nel suon del trino spiro is a relative clause (che + imperfect facea) modifying mischio, with facea an archaic imperfect of fare (= was made/was happening).
segments.paradiso-25-g127: sì come is a simile marker meaning "just as" or "in the same way that." Sì is the adverb "so/thus," and come is the comparative/conjunctive "as/like."
segments.paradiso-25-g128: per + infinitive (per cessar) expresses purpose or cause here: "to cease/for stopping." fatica o rischio are the coordinated objects "toil or danger/risks." The infinitive cessar is an older form (modern Italian: cessare).
segments.paradiso-25-g129: li remi = the oars; li is an archaic plural definite article (modern i or gli), and remi is the plural noun "oars." Here they are the grammatical subject of the following short clause.
segments.paradiso-25-g130: pria ne l’acqua ripercossi is a temporal/relative phrase modifying the action of the oars: pria = "before/first" (archaic adverb), ne l’acqua = "in/into the water" (ne + article), and ripercossi is a past-form meaning "were struck back" or "struck back" (agreeing with the plural remi); the sequence indicates the oars' motion in the rowing simile. Note that some lemmas here are archaic or irregular in the provided list.
segments.paradiso-25-g131: tutti si posano al sonar d’un fischio is the main result clause of the simile: tutti (all) is the subject, si posano is a pronominal verb (present tense meaning "they settle/cease/come to rest"), and al sonar d’un fischio is a prepositional phrase "at the sound of a whistle" (al = a + il "at the," sonar used as a noun-like infinitive "the sounding," d’un fischio = "of a whistle").
segments.paradiso-25-g132: Ahi quanto is an exclamatory opening meaning "Alas how..."; ne la mente means "in my mind" (ne = in), and mi commossi is the poet's emotional statement in the past tense: commossi is the first-person past (passato remoto) of the reflexive verb commuoversi, so "I was moved/touched."
segments.paradiso-25-g133: quando mi volsi = "when I turned"; volsi is the passato remoto (I turned myself). per veder Beatrice uses per + infinitive to express purpose or intent: "to see Beatrice" (per veder = in order to see).
segments.paradiso-25-g134: per non poter veder uses per + infinitive to introduce the circumstance/cause: "because/being unable to see." non negates the infinitive poter (to be able), so the phrase means "for not being able to see."
segments.paradiso-25-g135: benché io fossi presso di lei is a concessive clause: benché (although) requires the imperfect subjunctive here (io fossi = "though I were/though I was"); presso di lei means "near her/close to her." The subjunctive yields the sense "even though I was near her."
segments.paradiso-25-g136: e nel mondo felice is an exclamatory concluding phrase: e = "and," nel mondo = "in the world," and felice modifies mondo as "happy/blessed." The verb is omitted but understood from the previous clause: "and (yet) in the happy world!"
segments.paradiso-25-g113: A short nominative phrase: La donna mia = 'my lady.' La is the feminine definite article before donna; mia is a possessive agreeing with donna. Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' and sits after the noun for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-25-g114: This is a negative/result clause. Né però introduces 'nor, however'; piùe is a poetic form of più ('any more') strengthened by -e for the verse; mosser is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'moved' (here 3rd pl. past idea with archaic shape); la vista sua = 'her gaze/eyes' (la + noun + possessive after the noun); di stare attenta = an infinitival complement 'to remain attentive' (di + infinitive); poscia che prima le parole sue = temporal clause 'after (poscia) her words than before' (poscia che + comparative idea with prima). Overall: 'nor, however, did her gaze move any more from remaining attentive after her words than before.'
segments.paradiso-25-g115: Qual è colui = 'as is the man who / such is he who' — a comparative/illustrative formula. ch’ (che) introduces the relative clause. Adocchia is a poetical verb meaning to peer/squint/prepare one's sight (here in the present indicative); e connects the two verbs. S’argomenta is reflexive 'si argomenta' (he deliberates/arguments himself) — 's’' is the reflexive clitic; argomenta is present indicative meaning 'ponders' or 'discusses.' Di vedere eclissar lo sole un poco = 'about seeing the sun eclipse a little' where eclissar is the infinitive truncation eclissare (poetic elision).
segments.paradiso-25-g116: The conjunction che here continues/introduces the following explanatory clause ('who...'); it functions as a relative/conjunction linking the previous descriptive clause to the consequence.
segments.paradiso-25-g117: Per + infinitive (per veder) expresses purpose or the means 'in order to see' or 'for seeing.' Veder is the poetic infinitive (vedere) with final -e dropped for the verse.
segments.paradiso-25-g118: Non vedente diventa = 'he becomes not-seeing' (i.e., becomes unable to see). Diventa is 3rd person singular present indicative of diventare 'becomes'; non vedente is a predicative adjective/participle phrase 'not-seeing' or 'blind.' The clause describes an ironic effect: in trying to see one becomes unable to see.
segments.paradiso-25-g119: Tal mi fec’ ïo = 'Such (was) I made' i.e., 'such I became.' Fec' is the contracted past form of fece/feci (archaic poetic elision) and ïo is 'io' (I) placed after the verb for emphasis; mi is the reflexive or indirect object clitic. A quell’ ultimo foco = 'at that last flame/torch' (a + quell' + noun). Mentre che detto fu = 'while it was said' (mentre che + passato 'fu' 'was'); detto functions as a past participle or 'the saying' (what was said).
segments.paradiso-25-g120: Direct speech introduced. Perché = 'why.' T’abbagli = ti abbagli (ti + abbagli): a second-person present form 'you dazzle/you blind yourself' (the clitic t’ = 'ti' attaches to the verb). Per veder = 'in order to see' (purpose). Cosa che qui non ha loco = 'a thing that here has no place' — a relative clause giving the object: 'something that has no place here' (non ha loco is an idiom meaning 'is not appropriate/does not belong here').
segments.paradiso-25-g121: In terra = 'in the earth' or 'on earth' (locative phrase). È terra il mio corpo = copular construction with a predicate nominative: 'my body is earth.' The order puts the predicate before the subject for emphasis; è is present indicative 'is.'
segments.paradiso-25-g122: E saragli tanto con li altri = 'and it will be so / it will be to it so much with the others.' Saragli is an archaic/fused form: sarà (future of essere) + clitic gli (to it/him) contracted, giving a dative flavor 'will be for it'; tanto = 'so much/so' and con li altri = 'with the others.' The clause says the body will be among the others to that degree.
segments.paradiso-25-g123: Che introduces a result/consequence clause: 'that.' 'Il numero nostro' = 'our number' (the arithmetic/count of us). Con l’etterno proposito = 'with the eternal purpose' (proposito as 'purpose/intention' governed by con). S’agguagli is the reflexive present/subjunctive-style form of agguagliare (s'agguagliare) meaning 'become equalized' or 'be made equal'; in older usage this form expresses the result: 'will be made equal to / will match.'
segments.paradiso-25-g124: Con le due stole nel beato chiostro = prepositional phrase 'with the two stoles in the blessed cloister' (con + article + noun; nel = in + il). Son = poetic form of sono 'are' (3rd pl. present). Le due luci sole = 'the two lights alone/sole lights' (sole here qualifies luci, 'sole' in Dante can mean 'solitary' or 'guiding light'); che saliro = relative clause 'that I shall ascend' where saliro is the archaic future (salirò) of salire. The clause as a whole states the location and identity of the two lights and adds a relative remark about ascending.
segments.paradiso-25-g125: E questo apporterai nel mondo vostro = direct address with future second-person verb apporterai 'you will bring.' Questo is the demonstrative object 'this'; nel mondo vostro = 'into your world' or 'to your world' (vostro addressing those in the earthly realm). The future tense indicates a command/prediction about what the addressee will carry back to their world.
segments.paradiso-25-g101: Poscia (archaic for poscia/poi) means "afterward"; the phrase "Poscia tra esse un lume si schiarì" places the prepositional phrase first ("among them") and then the grammatical subject "un lume". "Si schiarì" is passato remoto, 3rd person singular, reflexive use of schiarire meaning "brightened" or "grew clear." "Sì che" introduces the result clause that follows.
segments.paradiso-25-g102: "Se" here is the conditional conjunction "if" introducing the protasis; the elided article "’l" = "il." "Cancro" is the personified zodiac sign (the Crab). "Avesse" is the congiuntivo imperfetto (imperfect subjunctive) of avere used in classical/poetic Italian in the dependent clause of a conditional (paired with the conditional in the apodosis below). "Un tal cristallo" = "such a crystal," with "tal" (tale) meaning "such/that kind of."
segments.paradiso-25-g103: "L’inverno avrebbe" uses the conditional present (avrebbe = would have) to give the result introduced by "sì che." The phrase "un mese d’un sol dì" is a poetic/hyperbolic expression: literally "a month of one single day," where "sol" is the poetic form of "solo" (= only).
segments.paradiso-25-g104: This string of verbs (surge, va, entra) is 3rd-person singular present and lists successive actions in a vivid, poetic sequence: "rises and goes and enters." The coordinating conjunctions "e/ed" connect the verbs; "in ballo" = "into the dance" and "vergine lieta" is a descriptive apposition "a joyful maiden."
segments.paradiso-25-g105: "Sol" here is the poetic/archaic adverb meaning "only/merely" (sol ~ solo); "per fare onore" is an infinitival purpose phrase, literally "to do/make honor" (= to pay honour); "a la novizia" identifies the person honoured (to/for the novice).
segments.paradiso-25-g106: A short negative prepositional phrase: "non per alcun fallo" = "not for any fault/ not because of any misdeed." After the negation, "alcun" takes the sense "any." The whole phrase denies that the motive is blame.
segments.paradiso-25-g107: In "così vid' io lo schiarato splendore venire..." the verb "vid'" (contracted vid' = vidi) precedes the subject "io" (inversion common in poetry). The construction "vidi + object + infinitive" (= "I saw the splendour come") is literary. "Venire a' due" = "come to the two," and the relative clause "che si volgieno a nota qual conveniesi..." contains "si volgieno" (they turn themselves) and an indirect clause with the imperfect subjunctive "conveniesi" (what would be fitting), functioning as an indirect question about what suited their ardent love.
segments.paradiso-25-g108: "Misesi" is passato remoto, 3rd person singular reflexive of mettere/misersi = "he set himself/took his place." "Lì nel canto e ne la rota" places location(s): "there in the canto (chorus/part of the circle) and in the rota (the round/dance)." "Ne" here is locative (“in the/within the”).
segments.paradiso-25-g109: The clause "e la mia donna in lor tenea l’aspetto" has subject "la mia donna" before the verb for emphasis. "Tenea" is imperfect indicative of tenere = "kept/held," and "l'aspetto" in this idiom means her attention/gaze. "In lor" = "on them."
segments.paradiso-25-g110: "Pur come" = "even/just as." The comparison "sposa tacita e immota" likens my lady to a bride who is "tacita" (silent/unspoken) and "immota" (motionless), a conventional simile stressing quiet reverence.
segments.paradiso-25-g111: "Questi è colui che giacque..." is a demonstrative identification: "This is he who lay..." "Giacque" (passato remoto of giacere) = "lay." "Sopra ’l petto del nostro pellicano" is a poetic metaphor: the pelican is a symbol (often of Christ or self‑sacrifice) and "nostro" marks it as the community's emblem.
segments.paradiso-25-g112: "E questi fue" = "and this one was" (fue = passato remoto of essere). The phrase "di su la croce" = "from upon the cross" (poetic ordering). "Al grande officio eletto" uses the past participle "eletto" (chosen) to indicate that he was chosen for the high/great office (officio = duty/office).
segments.paradiso-25-g84: Indi is an adverb meaning 'then' used to sequence the narrative. Spirò is the passato remoto (third person singular) of spirare meaning 'he breathed' or 'he sighed' — here a brief stage direction introducing direct speech.
segments.paradiso-25-g85: This long noun phrase names the motive: L' amore = 'the love' (article + noun). Ond' is an archaic relative/relational form meaning 'from/whence/ by which' and is cliticized to the following vowel; ïo is the emphatic first-person pronoun 'I' (trema marks a metrical hiatus). Avvampo is present indicative 'I blaze/ I am inflamed' used in a relative clause with ond' — literally 'the love from which I blaze.' Ancor = 'still'; ver' is an archaic/poetic contraction of verso 'toward' (not among the listed senses); la virtù is the feminine noun 'the virtue'; che mi seguette is a relative clause with seguette as passato remoto 'followed me'; infin la palma e a l'uscir del campo = 'as far as the palm and at the leaving/exit of the field' (infin = until/as far as, palma = palm/laurel/victory, a l'uscir = 'at the going-out/exit').
segments.paradiso-25-g86: Vuol is the third-person singular of volere 'wishes/wants' and introduces a subordinate clause with the subjunctive. Ch'io respiri is the subjunctive present (that I breathe/utter) required by vuol; here respiri functions idiomatically as 'that I speak/address' (literally 'breathe to you'). A te = 'to you' (indirect object). Che ti dilette is a relative clause modifying 'te' — ti = 'you' (reflexive/indirect), dilette is an older inflection meaning 'you delight' or 'you take pleasure' (the sense is 'you who delight in her'), di lei = 'in/with regard to her.'
segments.paradiso-25-g87: Ed = 'and'. Emmi a grato is an archaic impersonal or semi-idiomatic expression meaning 'and it pleases me' or 'and I am grateful' (emmi here is an old contracted form — functionally equivalent to 'mi è' or 'mi è caro'); a grato = 'pleasing' to me. Che tu diche is a subordinate clause with che + present subjunctive/indicative form meaning 'that you say' (diche = 'you say/tell'); quello che la speranza ti ’mpromette = 'what hope promises you' (quello che = 'that which', la speranza = 'the hope', ti ’mpromette = 'promises you' — ’mpromette is written with an elided initial consonant but is the modern promettere).
segments.paradiso-25-g88: E io is a simple narrative tag 'and I', introducing the speaker's reply. The pronoun io is emphatic/prefatory before the reported speech.
segments.paradiso-25-g89: Le nove = 'the new ones' (here 'the Gospels' or 'the new [testament writings]') and le scritture antiche = 'the ancient writings/scriptures'; pongon is an older form of pongono (third person plural present) 'they set/pose'; lo segno = 'the sign' (lo + noun). Word order is subject (the writings) + verb (pongon) + direct object (lo segno).
segments.paradiso-25-g90: Ed esso lo mi addita: ed = 'and'; esso = 'it' (referring back to the sign or text); lo mi addita is an archaic clitic order (lo + mi before the verb) meaning 'it points it out to me' — addita is third person singular present 'points out'. Note the older placement of object and indirect-object clitics (lo mi) rather than the modern mi lo.
segments.paradiso-25-g91: De l’anime = 'about/concerning the souls' (de = of/about). Che introduces the relative clause 'that God has made friends'; Dio = 'God'; s' ha fatte amiche is reflexive construction: s' (si) + ha (auxiliary) + fatte (past participle, agreeing in gender/number with anime) + amiche (predicative adjective 'friendly'/'friends'). Literally 'God has made the souls friends (to himself).' The participle fatte agrees with the feminine plural anime.
segments.paradiso-25-g92: Dice Isaia = 'Isaiah says' (dice = third person singular present 'says'); che introduces the content clause. Ciascuna vestita = 'each one, clothed' (vestita is a past participle used adjectivally); ne la sua terra = 'in her own land' (ne + the separated article is an archaic form of nella); fia is the future/subjunctive form (here future: 'will be'); di doppia vesta = 'of a double garment' (doppia vesta = double robe).
segments.paradiso-25-g93: e la sua terra è questa dolce vita is a copular clause: e = 'and'; la sua terra = 'her/its land'; è = present of essere 'is'; questa dolce vita = 'this sweet life' (predicate nominative). The clause identifies the 'land' mentioned above with the present blessed life.
segments.paradiso-25-g94: e 'l tuo fratello assai vie più digesta — e = 'and'; 'l tuo fratello = 'your brother'; assai = 'very/much'; vie più is an archaic adverbial intensifier meaning 'far more' or 'much more' (not the literal 'ways' sense of via); digesta is a past participle used figuratively meaning 'at ease/settled' or 'well-assimilated' ('more ready/experienced').
segments.paradiso-25-g95: là dove tratta de le bianche stole = 'there where one deals with/regards the white stoles': là dove = 'there where'; tratta (third person present) = 'treats/handles/discusses'; de le bianche stole = 'about the white stoles/robes' (de + article again an archaic separation rather than nella).
segments.paradiso-25-g96: questa revelazion ci manifesta = 'this revelation shows/reveals to us': questa revelazion = 'this revelation' (subject); ci = indirect-object clitic 'to us'; manifesta = third person singular present 'reveals/shows'. Word order places the indirect-object clitic before the verb, as in standard Italian.
segments.paradiso-25-g97: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. Prima is an adverb meaning 'before' or 'earlier' (temporal), placed after the conjunction for rhythmic/poetic emphasis; together they mark a preceding moment in the narrative.
segments.paradiso-25-g98: Appresso is an adverb meaning 'near' / 'close to' (here: near in time/place), followed by the prepositional phrase al fin d’este parole = 'at the end of these words'; al is the contraction of a (to/at) + il, d’ is the elided form of di, and este is the archaic/dialectal form of 'these'.
segments.paradiso-25-g99: “Sperent in te” is a quoted optative/subjunctival 3rd person plural (present subjunctive sense: 'may they hope in you'). Di sopr' a noi is an inverted/contracted locution for di sopra a noi ('from/above us'), and s’udì is si udì (impersonal/passive si) + udì (passato remoto 'was heard') meaning 'was heard'. Note the elisions: sopr' = sopra, d' = di, s'udì = si udì.
segments.paradiso-25-g100: A che here is a relative/conjunctive phrase 'to which / whereupon'; rispuoser is an archaic spelling of the 3rd person plural preterite (risposero) 'answered/responded', and tutte le carole is the feminine plural subject 'all the carols' (i.e. all the choirs/songs) agreeing with the plural verb.
segments.paradiso-25-g50: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' or 'so' modifying the whole sentence. Seguì is the passato remoto (simple past) of seguire, 'followed'; in narrative poetry this past-tense verb states a completed action. '’l' is the contracted definite article (il) before secondo lume; secondo is the ordinal 'second' modifying lume; lume here means a tangible luminous presence (a light, often a guiding light). Ancora here functions adverbially as 'again' / 'also', indicating another light follows in addition to a previous one.
segments.paradiso-25-g51: E is the conjunction 'and' linking this clause to what precedes. Quella pïa is a demonstrative + adjective phrase 'that pious one' (pïa = pious, spelled with diaeresis in the text). Che introduces a relative clause 'who/which'. Guidò is passato remoto 'guided' (3rd sing.), taking as its direct object 'le penne de le mie ali' — an archaic genitive construction (de le = delle) meaning 'the feathers of my wings'. A + così alto volo is a prepositional phrase ('to such a high flight'); così modifies alto.
segments.paradiso-25-g52: A la risposta 'to the reply/answer' introduces what follows; così 'thus' frames the manner of the anticipation. Mi is the clitic object pronoun 'me' (direct object of prevenne as used idiomatically), and prevenne is the passato remoto of prevenire used here to mean 'anticipated' or 'answered beforehand'. The whole phrase means 'she/it anticipated me with this answer.'
segments.paradiso-25-g53: La Chiesa militante is the fixed theological phrase 'the Church militant' (the earthly Church). Alcun figliuolo non ha is a negated construction: alcun with non typically yields 'not any' or 'no ...', so the phrase means 'has no son/child' or 'has no member with...'; con più speranza means 'with greater/more hope' (con + noun). Grammatically, the negation non scopes over avere (ha).
segments.paradiso-25-g54: Com’ è scritto is the idiom 'as it is written' (com' = come). Nel Sol che raggia tutto nostro stuolo is a poetic locution: nel Sol literally 'in the Sol/Sun' (here the celestial Sun imagery) + relative clause che raggia 'that radiates/shines' + tutto nostro stuolo 'over our whole host'. The order is typical poetic word order with the relative clause after the head noun.
segments.paradiso-25-g55: Però li è conceduto che ... is a formal impersonal construction: 'therefore it is granted to him that ...' Here li functions as an indirect-object clitic 'to him' (archaic placement before the verb), è conceduto is a passive/impersonal perfect participle used impersonally. Vegna is present subjunctive (vegna) of venire used after che with the sense 'that he may come' (a concessive/optative use). D’Egitto vegna in Ierusalemme per vedere = 'that he may come from Egypt to Jerusalem to see.'
segments.paradiso-25-g56: Anzi che here means 'before' (archaic/conjunctive use). Che introduces the subordinate clause. 'L militar' (the article + militar) stands for 'the military [service/obligation]'; sia is present subjunctive of essere (3rd sing.) used after che in a subordinate clause, and prescritto is the past participle 'prescribed/appointed'. Together: 'before the military service is prescribed to him.'
segments.paradiso-25-g57: Li is the archaic plural definite article (modern Italian: 'i' or 'gli' depending on gender) used here before altri; 'Li altri due punti' = 'The other two points.' Word order places the article before altri for emphasis, as in older Italian.
segments.paradiso-25-g58: Che introduces a relative clause 'which'. Non per sapere son dimandati is literally 'are not asked for (the purpose of) knowing' — son is the archaic present plural 'sono' (they are), dimandati is the past participle used in a passive sense 'asked/put forward'. Per + infinitive noun 'sapere' marks purpose ('for the sake of knowing').
segments.paradiso-25-g59: Ma perch' ei rapporti uses perche (here purpose 'so that') followed by ei (an archaic 3rd-person pronoun 'he') and rapporti, present subjunctive/optative form of rapportare used to express purpose ('that he may report'). Quanto questa virtù t’è in piacere is an inverted clause: quanto = 'how much', questa virtù = 'this virtue', t' è in piacere = 'is in pleasure to you' i.e. 'pleases you' (t' = clitic indirect object 'to you').
segments.paradiso-25-g60: A lui lasc' io inverts the normal modern order (lascio a lui) for poetic effect; lasc' is the contracted first-person present 'lascio' ('I leave/entrust') with the clitic vowel dropped before io. The phrase means 'I leave [those points] to him.'
segments.paradiso-25-g61: Ché introduces the reason 'for/because'. Non li saran forti is 'they will not be strong to/for him' — saran is the archaic future (saranno) and li is the clitic referring back to the points or to him (dative use), here indicating 'for him' or 'to him'. Né di iattanza pairs with né to mean 'nor of iattanza'; iattanza is an older noun (contextually 'vanity/boast' or potentially 'ill-omen/misfortune' depending on medieval usage) and completes the negative clause.
segments.paradiso-25-g62: Ed elli a ciò risponda is a jussive/optative clause: ed 'and' + elli (here 'he') + a ciò 'to that' + risponda, present subjunctive used with imperative force meaning 'let him answer to that' or 'may he answer that'. The use of the subjunctive for an exhortation is common in Dante's register.
segments.paradiso-25-g63: E la grazia di Dio ciò li comporti is a final wish: e 'and' + la grazia di Dio 'the grace of God' + ciò 'this/that' + li comporti, where comporti is present subjunctive (3rd sing.) of comportare expressing hope/wish 'may (God's) grace bring this to/for him' or 'may God's grace sustain him in it'. The clitic li again indicates 'to him' or 'for him'.
segments.paradiso-25-g64: Come introduces a simile 'like/as'. Discente is a present participle used as a noun 'a learner'. Ch’ a dottor seconda is a compressed phrase 'who as second to a teacher' (a dottor = to/at a teacher; seconda = 'second' or 'subordinate'). Pronto e libente are coordinate adjectives 'ready and willing'. In quel ch’ elli è esperto uses quel + relative clause 'in that in which he is experienced' (ch’ = che, elli = archaic 'he'); esperto is a predicate adjective.
segments.paradiso-25-g65: Perché la sua bontà si disasconda is a purpose clause: perché here means 'so that' (not merely 'because'), la sua bontà 'his goodness' and si disasconda is a reflexive/passive-form verb (disascondare) in the subjunctive or present-tense jussive sense 'may be uncovered/revealed'. The reflexive si marks that the goodness becomes manifest of itself.
segments.paradiso-25-g66: Spene is the nominative noun 'Hope' (here used as the subject of the following clause). The punctuation and placement mark it as a vocative or the topic of the speaker's definition.
segments.paradiso-25-g67: Diss' io is the poet's compressed past-tense report: diss' (disse with contracted final vowel) is passato remoto 'I said', and io is the emphatic subject 'I'. The inversion is typical in verse.
segments.paradiso-25-g68: È uno attender certo de la gloria futura: è (is) + uno attender certo ('a certain waiting' or 'a certain expectation'); attender is the infinitive used as a noun (a gerundial/nominal use) meaning 'waiting/expectation'. De la gloria futura = 'of future glory' (de = archaic of). The adjective certo qualifies the noun phrase.
segments.paradiso-25-g69: Il qual produce introduces a relative clause: il qual = 'which' referring back to 'attender certo'. Produce is present indicative 'produces' (causes), and grazia divina e precedente merto lists its effects: 'divine grace and preceding merit' (precedente merto = merit that precedes, i.e., meritorious antecedent). Word order places the produced effects after the verb.

sentences.paradiso-25-s13: This light comes to me from many stars; but that one distilled it into my heart before he was the supreme singer of the supreme leader.
sentences.paradiso-25-s14: 'Let them hope in you,' she says in her tëodia, 'those who know your name'; and who does not know it, if he has my faith?
sentences.paradiso-25-s15: You distilled me, with her dripping, into the little flask afterward; so that I am full, and I pour back your rain upon others.
sentences.paradiso-25-s16: While I was saying this, inside the living breast of that fire a flash trembled, sudden and frequent, like lightning.
sentences.paradiso-25-s5: Then Beatrice, laughing, said: "Renowned life, through whom the largeness of our basilica was composed, make hope resound at this height: you know that you represent it as many times as Jesus showed the three greater caresses."
sentences.paradiso-25-s6: "Lift up your head and make sure of it yourself: for whatever comes here up from the mortal world must ripen in our rays."
sentences.paradiso-25-s7: This comfort of the second fire came to me; whereupon I lifted my eyes to the mountains that had before been bowed down by their too great weight.
sentences.paradiso-25-s8: "Since by grace He wills that you present yourself to our Emperor — nay, Death — in the most secret hall with his counts, so that, having seen the truth of this court, the hope which below so well inspires love may be strengthened in you and in others by this, tell what it is, say how your mind blossoms from it, and say from whence it came to you."
sentences.paradiso-25-s1: If it should ever happen that the sacred poem — to which heaven and earth have lent a hand, so that it made me lean for many years — overcomes the cruelty that keeps me outside the fair sheepfold where I slept as a lamb, an enemy to the wolves that wage war on it; then with another voice, with another fleece I will return a poet, and at the font of my baptism I will take the cap; for it was there, in the faith that reckons souls to God, that I entered, and afterwards Peter turned my forehead because of it.
sentences.paradiso-25-s2: Then a light moved toward us from that sphere whence came the firstfruit that Christ left of his vicars; and my lady, full of joy, said to me, 'Look, look: here is the nobleman for whom down there Galicia is visited.'
sentences.paradiso-25-s3: Just as when the dove settles near its companion, each spreads affection to the other, turning and murmuring; so I saw one great prince received by another glorious prince, praising the food that there above feeds them.
sentences.paradiso-25-s4: But after the greeting had been completed, each one fell silent before me, so inflamed that it overwhelmed my face.
sentences.paradiso-25-s29: At those words the inflamed ring calmed itself, together with that sweet mingling which was made in the sound of the threefold breath, just as, to cease toil or danger, the oars — having been struck back into the water first — all rest at the sound of a single whistle.
sentences.paradiso-25-s30: Alas, how much I was moved in my mind when I turned to look at Beatrice, for not being able to see her, although I was near her and in the happy world!
sentences.paradiso-25-s25: My lady thus; nor, however, did her gaze move away from being attentive after her words any more than it had before.
sentences.paradiso-25-s26: As one who peers and debates whether to see the sun eclipse for a little while — who, in order to see, becomes unseeing — so I became at that last flame while it was said: 'Why do you blind yourself to see something that has no place here?'
sentences.paradiso-25-s27: On earth my body is earth, and it will be with the others to such an extent that our number will be made equal with the eternal purpose.
sentences.paradiso-25-s28: With the two stoles in the blessed cloister are the two lone lights that I shall mount; and you shall bring this into your world.
sentences.paradiso-25-s21: Afterward a light among them brightened so much that, if Cancer had such a crystal, winter would have a month of a single day.
sentences.paradiso-25-s22: And as a joyful maiden rises and goes and enters the dance — only to pay honour to the novice, not on account of any fault — so I saw the brightened splendour come to the two who turned toward the cue to learn what would be fitting for their ardent love.
sentences.paradiso-25-s23: He took his place there in the chorus and in the circle; and my lady kept her gaze on them, just like a silent and motionless bride.
sentences.paradiso-25-s24: "This is he who lay upon the breast of our pelican, and this one was, from on the cross, chosen for the great office."
sentences.paradiso-25-s17: Then he sighed: "The love by which I still blaze toward the virtue that followed me as far as the palm and at the field's exit wants that I speak to you who take delight in her; and it would please me that you say what hope promises you."
sentences.paradiso-25-s18: And I said: "The Gospels and the ancient writings set the sign, and it points it out to me concerning the souls that God has made friends."
sentences.paradiso-25-s19: Isaiah says that each one, clothed in her own land, will be in a double garment: and her land is this sweet life; and your brother, much more fully at ease, — there, where matters of the white stoles are handled, — this revelation shows to us."
sentences.paradiso-25-s20: And before, near the end of these words, “May they hope in you” was heard above us; to which all the carols responded.
sentences.paradiso-25-s9: Thus the second light followed as well.
sentences.paradiso-25-s10: And that pious one who guided the feathers of my wings to so high a flight anticipated me with this answer: "The Church militant has no son with greater hope, as it is written in the Sun that shines over our whole host; therefore it is granted to him that he may come from Egypt to Jerusalem to see, before the military service is prescribed to him."
sentences.paradiso-25-s11: The other two points, which are not asked for the sake of knowing but so that he may report how much this virtue is pleasing to you, I leave to him; for they will not be strong for him nor (cause) misfortune; and let him answer to that, and may the grace of God sustain him in it.
sentences.paradiso-25-s12: Like a learner who, as second to a teacher, is ready and willing in what he is experienced, so that his goodness may be revealed, "Hope," I said, "is a certain waiting for future glory, which produces divine grace and preceding merit."
